summary: My political involvement started about twenty or thirty years ago when I began driving people to the polls under the sponsorship of the Durham Committee on Negro Affairs, now called the Durham Committee on the Affairs of Black People. This committee has played a key role in black political development in Durham, acting as a unifying force by interviewing candidates, making recommendations, and organizing a bloc vote. The committee is open to everyone in the black community and operates nonpartisan, including Republicans in their sample ballots. John Wheeler, president of Mechanics and Farmers Bank, has been chairman for at least sixteen years, following John Stuart who served before him.

I first ran for the North Carolina House of Representatives in 1964, the year I graduated from law school, but I lost that race as well as in 1966 and 1968. I didn't run in 1970 but came back in 1972 and won, securing one of three seats with no opposition in the general election. I was re-elected in 1974, again facing no opposition in the general election. My experience in the House was part of my broader political journey in Durham and North Carolina.

Durham has a reputation as one of the most politically active cities in North Carolina and the South, especially for black political development. The city benefits from a strong black economic base, including institutions like banks, insurance companies, and the university, which supports political activism. The Durham Committee on Negro Affairs has been instrumental in organizing the black community, facilitating voter turnout, and influencing elections. The black vote in Durham County can be around fourteen to sixteen thousand, with turnout often exceeding that of white voters.

John Wheeler has been the chairman of the Durham Committee on Negro Affairs for at least sixteen years. He is also the president of Mechanics and Farmers Bank, which is one of the strong black institutions in Durham. Wheeler took over the chairmanship after John Stuart left to join the city council. His leadership has been central to the committee's role in organizing the black community politically.

Mechanics and Farmers Bank is one of the strong black institutions in Durham that supports the community economically and politically. John Wheeler, the bank's president, has been chairman of the Durham Committee on Negro Affairs for many years, linking the bank closely to political activism. The bank represents part of the independent black economic base in Durham, which includes other institutions like insurance companies and the university.

John Stuart was the chairman of the Durham Committee on Negro Affairs before John Wheeler took over. He left the chairmanship when he was elected to the city council. His leadership helped shape the committee's early political influence in Durham's black community.

Ike Andrews ran for office in 1972 against Jack Hawk and won by about eleven hundred votes, largely due to solid black support in Durham. Our efforts concentrated on turning out the black vote, which proved decisive in his victory. When Jack Hawk challenged the vote, only the black precincts in Durham County were contested, but the challenge failed, confirming Ike's win. Ike now feels that Durham has adopted him because of this strong backing.

Jack Hawk was Ike Andrews's opponent in the 1972 race. After losing by about eleven hundred votes, Hawk challenged the vote, but the only precincts contested were the black precincts in Durham County. The challenge was unsuccessful, and Ike Andrews retained his victory. This race highlighted the power of the black vote in Durham politics.

Georgia was mentioned as an example of a state with very active black political organization. While North Carolina was attempting to organize statewide black political groups, Georgia already had a strong presence. This comparison helped illustrate the challenges and potential for black political organization in North Carolina.

Mississippi was referenced alongside other Southern states like Georgia, South Carolina, and Alabama when discussing black political activity in the South. The mention served to contextualize North Carolina's political environment within the broader Southern region. It highlighted the varying degrees of black political organization across these states.

South Carolina was mentioned as part of a group of Southern states, including Georgia, Mississippi, and Alabama, known for black political activity. This was in the context of discussing the efforts to organize black political groups in North Carolina and the South more broadly. South Carolina's political environment provided a regional backdrop for these discussions.

Alabama was cited along with Georgia, Mississippi, and South Carolina when talking about black political activism in the South. The mention was part of a broader conversation about the challenges and progress in organizing black political groups across Southern states. Alabama's experience helped frame North Carolina's own political efforts.

The Council of State in North Carolina was mentioned as a potential catalyst for forming a statewide black elected officials' organization. Currently, there are no black members in the Council of State, and without such statewide elected officials, organizing a statewide black political group has been difficult. If a black candidate were elected to the Council of State, it might provide the leadership needed to unify black elected officials across North Carolina.

There was no active effort yet to organize a statewide black elected officials' organization in North Carolina, and I did not see one coming soon. The National Black Elected Officials Organization was not directly mentioned in the transcript, but the discussion about organizing black elected officials at the state level implied that such efforts were still in early stages. The lack of statewide black elected officials in North Carolina has been a barrier to forming a strong organization.
